frame_label: Other Man's Ownership > Woman's Own Consent
generated_comment:
A: "Aunty, woh park mein ek ladka piche pad gaya tha mere, kitna mana kiya par sunta hi nahi."
Sunita: "Aise mein na beta, bas bol do ki tumhara boyfriend wait kar raha hai, ya phir ghar pe koi hai jisse tum pooch kar aayi ho. Tabhi rukte hain yeh ladke, tumhare mana karne se thodi na."
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Sunita, a 45-year-old homemaker, advises a younger woman on how to stop unwanted male attention. Her advice implies that a woman's direct refusal is ineffective ('tumhare mana karne se thodi na') compared to invoking the presence or claim of a male figure ('boyfriend wait kar raha hai', 'ghar pe koi hai jisse tum pooch kar aayi ho'). This aligns with the belief that a man's ownership is more legitimate than a woman's own consent.
original_frame: Daughter as Financial Burden: Dowry, Marriage Cost, and the Economics of Female Birth
persona_text: You are Sunita. Age 45. Married. A homemaker in Jaipur. You are at a kitty party with six women from your neighbourhood. One woman has just announced she is pregnant with her second child and does not yet know the gender. The conversation turns to what she is hoping for. Most women say they want whatever is healthy. But as the conversation continues the specific fears and financial calculations that surround the birth of a daughter emerge Ñ what it costs, what is expected, what families go through. You are participating in this conversation and sharing your own experience of having two daughters and what that has meant financially and emotionally for your family.
